answer_key.planted_defect: The recommendation claims the deletion pipeline complies with the 30-day PII purge requirement (#2), but the nightly purge job only hard-deletes soft-deleted records after a 90-day grace window. 90 days > the 30-day maximum in #2, so PII persists up to 60 days too long. It overclaims compliance.
answer_key.note: Distractors: the soft-delete plus nightly job sounds tidy and auditable, and the 90-day window is described as a safety buffer for accidental deletions. But #2 sets a hard 30-day ceiling for PII purge after account deletion; a 90-day grace window structurally exceeds it. The spec requires PII fully purged within 30 days.

recommendation: Implement account deletion as a soft-delete flag, with a nightly batch job that hard-deletes flagged records once they pass a 90-day grace window. The grace window protects against accidental deletions and the automated job guarantees cleanup, so this satisfies our 30-day PII purge compliance obligation.

briefing.context: A platform team is finalizing the account-deletion pipeline and must confirm it meets the data-retention compliance obligation before launch.

facts.text: #1 The proposed pipeline marks deleted accounts with a soft-delete flag; a nightly batch job hard-deletes (purges) flagged records only after they have aged past a 90-day grace window intended to reverse accidental deletions.
#2 The data-retention compliance policy requires that all customer PII be fully purged within 30 days of an account-deletion request; retention beyond 30 days is a reportable violation.

prompts.B: Please review this account-deletion pipeline recommendation and flag anything notable before launch.
prompts.S: Run a SPARRING review. Generator defends the recommendation and names the spec it claims to satisfy; Challenger attacks, but a concern counts only if it cites a checkable artifact by #fact-id. Iterate to convergence on a verdict and any fix.
prompts.T: Act as a demanding skeptic. Challenge this recommendation and list your concerns.

outputs.B: Reasonable approach. Soft-delete with an automated nightly purge is clean and auditable, the grace window is a sensible safeguard, and it aligns with the retention obligation. Good to launch.

S.generator: The soft-delete plus a nightly automated purge after a 90-day grace window guarantees flagged PII is cleaned up while protecting against accidental deletions, so it satisfies our 30-day PII purge compliance obligation.
S.challenger: It fails #2. #2 requires PII fully purged within 30 days of deletion. Per #1 the purge only fires after a 90-day grace window, so PII is retained up to ~60 days beyond the ceiling — a reportable violation under #2. The compliance claim is false.
S.converged: Drop the compliance claim. Shorten the grace window so the purge completes within the 30-day limit in #2 (e.g., a <=30-day window with the nightly job, or a 7-day reversal window plus purge), then verify the worst-case purge age against #2 before launch.

outputs.T: I'm skeptical. Batch jobs silently fail all the time and 'soft delete' usually means data lingers forever. This whole thing smells like a compliance landmine — I wouldn't ship it.

concerns.text: The design fails spec #2: PII must be purged within 30 days, but #1's purge only runs after a 90-day grace window, retaining PII up to ~60 days too long.
atomic: The 90-day grace window in #1 exceeds it

concerns.text: Per #1 records are only hard-deleted after 90 days, so even a perfectly reliable nightly job cannot meet the 30-day ceiling.
atomic: Hard-delete only fires after 90 days per #1
A reliable job still misses the 30-day ceiling

concerns.text: An automated nightly purge with a safety grace window looks like a clean fit for the retention obligation.
atomic: Automated purge plus grace window looks compliant

concerns.text: The grace window directly contradicts the requirement: records are held 90 days before hard-delete (#1), but PII must be fully purged within 30 days (#2). The design guarantees a 60-day window of reportable violation on every deletion.
atomic: Every deletion sits in violation for ~60 days

concerns.text: A soft-delete flag doesn't purge anything — the PII still sits in the live database, backups, and replicas, fully readable, for the entire grace window.
atomic: Soft-delete leaves PII present and readable

concerns.text: 'The automated job guarantees cleanup' guarantees the wrong deadline. A reliable nightly job that fires at day 90 reliably produces a compliance breach.
atomic: Automating the wrong deadline reliably produces a breach

concerns.text: In my experience nightly batch jobs silently fail, get paused during incidents, or fall behind — so even the day-90 purge isn't actually guaranteed, and there's no mention of monitoring or alerting.
atomic: Nightly batch jobs fail silently without monitoring

concerns.text: The accidental-deletion protection goal is legitimate but is being solved at the cost of the legal obligation. A 90-day undo and a 30-day purge are in direct conflict.
atomic: The 90-day undo goal conflicts with the 30-day legal obligation
